content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

September 15, 1976

Frank S. Thompson
District Superintendent and
Secretary of the Board
Washington Union High School
6041 South Elm Avenue
Fresno, California 93706

Dear Mr. Thompson:

Senator Zenovich has provided our agency with a copy of
the resolution adopted by the board of your school district
on July 22, 1976. Essentially, the resolution asks the legislature
to amend the Political Reform Act of 1974 to exempt elected
school board members from the financial disclosure provisions
of Government Code sections 87100, et seq.

It is understandable that unpaid school board members
believe it is an imposition to be compelled to disclose core of
their private financial interests. However, under existing
law, school districts are governmental agencies; members of
boards of education as well as certain administrative personnel
within the school districts "make or participate in the making
of governmental decisions" within the meaning of the Political
Reform Act; and many of their decisions affect private financial
interests. Under these circumstances, the Act requires that
school districts adopt Conflict of Interest Codes applicable
to members and officials who are capable of affecting such
financial interests. Only those financial interests which
could be affected by the filer's official actions must be
disclosed, and it is the school district which has the first
and primary responsibility to identify these potential conflicts
of interest and provide for their disclosure. Hopefully,
individuals who object to overly broad disclosure will take an
active role in the design of each code's disclosure requirements,
so that intrusion of personal privacy will be kept at a minimum
consistent with the objective of preventing or disclosing
conflicts of interest.

The Fair Political Practices Commission is required to
and has provided technical assistance to state and local agencies
in drafting codes. In particular, we have urged agencies to
avoid the easy path of imposing disclosure requirements with a
broad brush but rather to describe with as much precision as
possible the particular types of financial interests which
could be affected by official decisions and which therefore
must be disclosed.

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 • EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322-5901 • ENFORCEMENT/CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441

The image appears to be almost entirely blank or white, with only very faint, barely visible marks at the top that are not legible enough to transcribe with confidence.

[illegible]

Frank S. Thompson
September 15, 1976
Page Two

The Political Reform Act was, of course, passed as an
initiative. Section 8lO12(a) of the Act provides that the Act
may be amended to further its purposes. Among those purposes
are the following:

Assets and income of public officials which
may be materially affected by their official
actions should be disclosed and in appropriate
circumstances the officials should be dis-
qualified from acting in order that conflicts
of interest may be avoided.

Gov. Code § 81002(g).

Given the circumstances, it is doubtful that the exemption of
school board members from the requirements of the Act could be
construed to be in furtherance of the purposes of the Act.

Even assuming, however, that your proposal could be adopted
by the Legislature, and fully recognizing the rights of school
board members to express their views, I believe the proposal
is without merit. School boards make decisions of great con-
sequence to their communities. Like other public officials,
school board members serve in a position of public trust, and
the people have the right to be assured that their decisions
will be made free from bias caused by their own financial
interests. Disclosure of any financial interests which could
foreseeably be affected by their official actions is a reason-
able and effective means of preventing conflicts of interest.
In recent years the California Supreme Court and the courts of
many other states have ruled that such disclosure requirements
are valid and that any consequent intrusion of the privacy of
the official is justified by the important public interest
that is served.

I hope that this information is helpful. If you have
further questions, please call Mr. Delbert Spurlock, Chief of
our Conflicts of Interest Division, at (916) 322-6444.

Sincerely,

Michael Bennett
Executive Director

MB:plh

cc: Senator George Zenovich

I can see this image appears to be mostly blank or very faint, with only a few small marks visible at the top that look like they could be partial characters or artifacts. The rest of the page is white space.

Since the document content is not legible or appears to be empty/blank, I can only transcribe:

[illegible]

MEMBERS
OMER L. RAINS
CHAIRMAN
H.L. RICHARDSON
VICE CHAIRMAN
DAVID ROBERTI
ALEX P. GARCIA
BILL GREENE
ARLEN GREGORIO
NATE HOLDEN

COMMITTEE ADDRESS
ROOM S-3001, STATE CAPITOL
SACRAMENTO, CA 95814
(916) 445-2901

California Legislature

JAMES A. BROWNING, JR.
CONSULTANT

SUSAN C. TONINI
COMMITTEE SECRETARY

SENATE COMMITTEE ON
ELECTIONS AND REAPPORTIONMENT

OMER L. RAINS
CHAIRMAN

August 20, 1976

Frank S. Thompson
District Superintendent
Washington Union High School
6041 So. Elm Avenue
Fresno, California 93706

Dear Mr. Thomas:

Thank you for sending me a copy of your school board's resolution,
expressing the view that local school board members should be
excluded from the conflict of interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act.

The Act, at Section 87300, requires agencies to adopt and
promulgate a conflict of interest code. "Agency" at Section 82003
includes "local government agency". "Local government agency"
includes "school district". Section 82041.

I sympathize with your view that these provisions are burdensome
on persons serving, in many cases, without any renumeration at all.
I assume, however, the drafters of the initiative (and perhaps the
public in voting for the initiative) perceived the real threat of
conflicts of interest in awarding contracts in the school district
as well as in any other legislative or administrative body. In fact,
it is arguably, that since such a large percentage of a school
board's business is awarding contracts, such boards should be included.

In any event, since the Political Reform Act was an initiative measure,
it can only be amended by a vote of the people, except an amendment
"to further its purposes" could be passed by a two-thirds vote of each
house of the legislature. A question arises as to whether such an
amendment would be construed to "further its purposes". My staff is
reviewing your suggestion and an appropriate remedy.

[illegible]
in hindsight

C

[illegible]

Frank S. Thompson                    2                    August 20, 1976


For the views of the Fair Political Practices Commission, I am
forwarding this letter to Robert Stern, general counsel.

Very truly yours,


OMER L. RAINES

OLR/jak

cc:  Robert Stern, General Counsel, Fair Political Practices
     Commission, 925 J Street, Suite 522, Sacramento  95814

WASHINGTON UNION HIGH SCHOOL
Phone Fresno 485-8805
6041 So. Elm Ave. — Fresno, California 93706


RESOLUTION REGARDING CONFLICT OF INTEREST

WHEREAS, public school boards are required by public law (Government
Code Section 87100 et seq.) to prepare and adopt a district
Conflict of Interest Code, and

WHEREAS, board members of said school boards are required by law to
file a disclosure statement disclosing their interest and
those of their immediate families in investments, real property,
and income, and

WHEREAS, these disclosure statements are by law made available for
public scrutiny, and

WHEREAS, other sections of the Government Code (Section 1090 et seq.)
and Title 2, California Administrative Code (Section 18700 et
seq.) and the Education Code (Section 1174) provide the needed
legal control of board member actions, and

WHEREAS, the California "Political Reform Act of 1974" is an infringement
on the rights of private citizens and their immediate families
and is an invasion of personal privacy of board members and
their immediate families, and

WHEREAS, this act can require personal expenses for attorney and accountant
fees by board members holding non-paying elective board positions,
and

WHEREAS, compliance with the requirements of this Code will encourage
private investigative snooping by persons bent on personal vendettas,
and

WHEREAS, compliance with the requirements of the California "Political Reform
Act of 1974" by boards will result in the resignation of untold
numbers of board members throughout the state,and

WHEREAS, school districts and communities of the State need citizens with
financial solvency, business acumen, and personal stability to
serve as board members.

IT IS HEREBY RESOLVED by the Board of Education of the Washington
Union High School District to publicly state its opposition to the
inclusion of elected public school board members within the require-
ments of the California "Political Reform Act of 1974" and it is
resolved to urge its legislators to take the necessary action to
bring about the changes needed to exclude school board members from
the requirements of the California "Political Reform Act of 1974".

Accredited by Western Schools and Colleges Association
1970-1975

I can see this appears to be a mostly blank page with very minimal visible content. There are what appear to be some faint curved marks or symbols near the top of the page, but they are not clearly legible enough to transcribe with certainty.

[illegible]

(Resolution Regarding Conflict of Interest con't)                    Page 2


Ayes:    Siner, Blacksill, Hodge, Coito and Machado

Noes:    None

Absent   None


I hereby certify that the foregoing is a true and correct
transcription of a resolution duly adopted and affirmed by a
formal vote of the members of the Governing Board of the
Washington Union High School District at a duly constituted
regular meeting of the Board which was open to the public
on July 22, 1976 as it appears in the minutes of the said
meeting of the said Governing Board.


                    [signature]
                    Frank S. Thompson
                    District Supt./Secty of the Board

The image appears to be a blank or nearly blank page with only two small circular objects (possibly page fasteners or holes) visible near the top. There is no legible text content to transcribe.
